text: The surgeon introduces a FIMM scope in the subumbilical position for visibility, followed by the removal of adhesions in the suprapubic region. A TNMM port is placed in the suprapubic space, and the camera is inserted, providing a clear view of the infracolic compartment, including the DIII and DIV sections. The DIII, superior mesenteric vein (SMV), and pancreas are visible, and the duodenum is being mobilized.

The surgeon retracts the colon upwards and holds a leaf of mesocolon, pulling the right colic artery downwards. Using a harmonic scalpel, the mesocolon leaf is dissected, followed by the duodenum. Care is taken to avoid injury to the right colic artery, middle colic artery, and the marginal arcade of droma. Blunt and sharp dissection techniques separate the mesocolon from the duodenum and the duodenum from its retroperitoneal attachments, completing the caucarization process.

The surgeon completes the mobilization of the final portion of the D-III segment of the duodenum, taking down retroperitoneal attachments at the D-II and D-III levels. The jejunum is traced from the duodenal jejunal flexure, and a suitable loop is positioned above the SMA-SMB, close to the mobilized D-II and D-III segments, preparing for a tension-free anastomosis.

The surgeon performs a double-layered hand-sewn continuous anastomosis between the duodenum and jejunum using a 3-0 Prolene suture. The outer layer involves rotating the duodenum and taking the first bite from the posterior side, while the jejunum is sutured close to the mesentery to prevent narrowing. The first bite is carefully knotted, and the smaller side of the suture is used for retraction, allowing clear visualization of the DII-DIII segment and jejunal loop. Both loops are brought close together by retracting, forming the fourth layer of the continuous suture.

The surgeon uses proline sutures to perform a series of bites on the posterior of the duodenum and close to the mesentery of the jejunum, ensuring the sutures are tightened without causing narrowing. The duodenum and jejunum are opened using a harmonic scalpel to a length of six to seven centimeters, avoiding posterior wall and thermal injury by rotating the scalpel so the inactive portion remains within the lumen.

The surgeon performs the second and third layers of continuous anastomosis using vicryl sutures. A corner suture is placed at the inferior corner of the jejunum for retraction, while a proline suture is used superiorly. The needle is introduced from outside to inside at the corner of the jejunum, allowing for retraction of the posterior wall. Common bites are taken between the duodenum and jejunum, completing the anastomosis.

The surgeon performs a continuous suturing technique from cephalic to caudal using vicryl sutures, ensuring each bite is tightened to achieve proper mucosal inversion. The sutures are tied to the initial corner suture, avoiding mucosal eversion. The technique is demonstrated with visible precision in a laparoscopic view.

The surgeon completes the final layer of the duodenal-jejunal bypass anastomosis using continuous proline sutures, ensuring the anastomosis is completely buried. Hemostasis is achieved with adequate suction, and a drain is placed near the anastomosis. Minimal fat is visible, allowing clear visualization of the vessels.
